title: Carmichaelia divaricata
intro: Carmichaelia divaricata, also known as the broom bignon or broom pea, is a species of flowering plant in the pea family (Fabaceae) native to New Zealand. The plant has been traditionally used in Maori medicine to treat a variety of health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and colds, due to its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral properties. The bioactive constituents of Carmichaelia divaricata include alkaloids, glycosides, and phenolic compounds, which are believed to contribute to its therapeutic effects. In herbal medicine, the plant is commonly prepared as a tea or a tincture, and is used to treat respiratory infections, digestive issues, and skin conditions, although its efficacy and safety have not been extensively scientifically studied.
taxonomy: Carmichaelia divaricata is classified within the family Fabaceae, commonly known as the pea or legume family. It belongs to the subfamily Faboideae, which contains most of the species within the Fabaceae family. The genus Carmichaelia is native to New Zealand and Australia, and it is often referred to as the New Zealand spider-flower or native pea. The species Carmichaelia divaricata is one of the many species within the Carmichaelia genus. Further classification includes the tribe Podalyrieae, to which Carmichaelia divaricata also belongs.
